description:
src/lib.rs:11-13 documents that the cache lock is never held across an .await point. The crate uses tokio::sync::Mutex anyway. Tokio's own documentation (https://docs.rs/tokio/latest/tokio/sync/struct.Mutex.html#which-kind-of-mutex-should-you-use) is explicit on this:

> Contrary to popular belief, it is ok and often preferred to use the ordinary Mutex from the standard library in asynchronous code... The primary use case for the async mutex is to provide shared mutable access to IO resources such as a database connection... The feature that the async mutex offers over the blocking mutex is the ability to keep it locked across an .await point.

Current cost of using tokio::sync::Mutex here:
- Every lock() call is async (must be .awaited), adding async machinery to a never-blocking critical section.
- Forces both .lock() sites in get() and put()/refresh() to live inside async fns even though the work is purely synchronous (memcpy + Instant compare).
- Slightly larger Mutex futures and a (very small) extra heap allocation per lock acquisition.

Fix: replace tokio::sync::Mutex<Option<CachedEntry>> with std::sync::Mutex<Option<CachedEntry>>. Drop the .await on lock() calls. The doc note at lib.rs:9-13 then describes the lock discipline accurately by construction, since std Mutex cannot be held across await. This is the canonical 'cache state behind a synchronous mutex inside an async wrapper' pattern.

Caveat: if a panicking guard is a concern, parking_lot::Mutex is poison-free, but std Mutex is fine here — the only operations under the lock are infallible (Option assignment, Instant comparison, Vec memcpy).
title: tokio::sync::Mutex used where std::sync::Mutex is idiomatic (lock never held across await)
